name: Rubidium
name_origin: From Latin 'rubidus' meaning 'deep red' or 'dark red', after the two prominent red lines in its emission spectrum, named by Robert Bunsen and Gustav Kirchhoff in 1861.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Rubidium is the 23rd most abundant element in Earth's crust at roughly 90 ppm, comparable to nickel or zinc. It does not occur free in nature but is found in small amounts in minerals such as lepidolite, pollucite, and carnallite.

compounds.name: Rubidium chloride
compounds.summary: A water-soluble ionic salt used in biochemistry research and as a non-radioactive marker.

compounds.name: Rubidium hydroxide
compounds.summary: A strong base similar to potassium hydroxide, used in some specialty chemistry applications.

compounds.name: Rubidium carbonate
compounds.summary: Used in special-purpose optical glasses to improve stability and reduce conductivity.

compounds.name: Rubidium superoxide
compounds.summary: A yellow-orange compound formed when rubidium burns in oxygen, of interest in oxygen-storage chemistry.

compounds.name: Rubidium iodide
compounds.summary: An ionic salt with applications in scintillation detectors and specialty optics.

uses: Atomic clocks (rubidium frequency standards) for GPS and telecommunications timing
Photocells and photoemitter devices due to ease of ionization
Specialty glasses and getters in vacuum tubes
Research applications in laser cooling and Bose-Einstein condensates
reactivity_summary: Rubidium is an extremely reactive alkali metal that ignites spontaneously in air and reacts violently, often explosively, with water to release hydrogen and form rubidium hydroxide. It must be stored under dry mineral oil or inert gas. It reacts vigorously with halogens and forms amalgams with mercury.
isotopes_summary: Rubidium has two naturally occurring isotopes: stable 85Rb (~72.17%) and the very long-lived radioactive 87Rb (~27.83%, half-life ~4.9x10^10 years), which beta-decays to 87Sr and is widely used in rubidium-strontium geochronology to date rocks. Numerous synthetic isotopes have been produced for research.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
